FI 362 — Day 1 Supplement

Asset Returns & Distributional Properties, Step by Step with Real Data

Author

Mehman Ismayilli

Published

June 22, 2026

How to use this notebook. It mirrors the Day 1 slides (FI362_Day01_FE) section by section, but computes every concept on real market data instead of stating it abstractly. Read the slide, then run the matching block here. All data lives in the sibling data/ folder and was built by data/download_data.R (daily Yahoo Finance prices, Jan 2000 – May 2026, adjusted for splits & dividends).

1 Setup & data

library(ggplot2)
library(moments)   # skewness(), kurtosis() -- we also code the Tsay versions by hand
library(tseries)   # jarque.bera.test()
library(zoo)
library(knitr)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

# Locate the data folder robustly (works whether rendered from this folder,
# the project root, or interactively).
data_dir <- local({
  cands <- c("../data", "data", file.path(getwd(), "data"))
  hit <- cands[file.exists(file.path(cands, "indices_adjclose.csv"))]
  if (!length(hit)) stop("Could not find the data/ folder. Run data/download_data.R first.")
  hit[1]
})

read_prices <- function(file) {
  df <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, file), check.names = FALSE)
  df$Date <- as.Date(df$Date)
  df
}

indices   <- read_prices("indices_adjclose.csv")   # daily, wide
stocks    <- read_prices("stocks_adjclose.csv")     # daily, wide
idx_month <- read_prices("indices_monthly.csv")     # month-end, wide
sp_month  <- read_prices("sp500_monthly.csv")       # month-end S&P 500
meta      <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "metadata.csv"))

kable(meta[, c("symbol","name","region","asset_class","rows","start","end")],
      caption = "The downloaded universe of global indices and stocks.")
The downloaded universe of global indices and stocks.
symbol name region asset_class rows start end
^GSPC SP500 United States Equity index 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^IXIC NASDAQ United States Equity index 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^DJI DowJones United States Equity index 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^FTSE FTSE100 United Kingdom Equity index 6719 2000-01-04 2026-05-29
^GDAXI DAX Germany Equity index 6747 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^FCHI CAC40 France Equity index 6784 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
^N225 Nikkei225 Japan Equity index 6589 2000-01-04 2026-06-01
^HSI HangSeng Hong Kong Equity index 6583 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
000001.SS SSEComposite China Equity index 6519 2000-01-04 2026-05-29
^KS11 KOSPI South Korea Equity index 6603 2000-01-04 2026-06-01
^BSESN Sensex India Equity index 6596 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
AAPL AAPL United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
MSFT MSFT United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
AXP AXP United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
CAT CAT United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29
SBUX SBUX United States Stock 6641 2000-01-03 2026-05-29

We will use the S&P 500 as our running example index and AAPL as our running example stock, but every computation generalises to any column.

# --- log / simple return helpers -------------------------------------------
lag1       <- function(x) c(NA, head(x, -1))       # tiny one-step lag, no deps
simple_ret <- function(p) p / lag1(p) - 1          # R_t = P_t/P_{t-1} - 1
log_ret    <- function(p) c(NA, diff(log(p)))      # r_t = ln P_t - ln P_{t-1}

# --- sample moments EXACTLY as defined on the slides (Tsay, T-1 scaling) ----
samp_stats <- function(x) {
  x  <- x[is.finite(x)]
  T  <- length(x)
  m  <- mean(x)
  s  <- sqrt(sum((x - m)^2) / (T - 1))             # sample sd
  sk <- sum((x - m)^3) / ((T - 1) * s^3)           # sample skewness
  ku <- sum((x - m)^4) / ((T - 1) * s^4)           # sample kurtosis
  data.frame(n = T, mean = m, sd = s, skew = sk,
             exkurt = ku - 3, min = min(x), max = max(x))
}

# --- normality test statistics (slides: skew, kurtosis, Jarque-Bera) --------
norm_tests <- function(x) {
  x  <- x[is.finite(x)]; T <- length(x)
  st <- samp_stats(x)
  t_sk <- st$skew   / sqrt(6  / T)                 # H0: S = 0
  t_ku <- st$exkurt / sqrt(24 / T)                 # H0: K - 3 = 0
  JB   <- t_sk^2 + t_ku^2                           # = S^2/(6/T) + (K-3)^2/(24/T)
  data.frame(skew = st$skew, t_skew = t_sk, p_skew = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(t_sk))),
             exkurt = st$exkurt, t_kurt = t_ku, p_kurt = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(t_ku))),
             JB = JB, p_JB = 1 - pchisq(JB, df = 2))
}

2 From prices to returns

Slides: “Asset Returns – One period”. A one-period simple gross return is \(1+R_t = P_t/P_{t-1}\); the simple net return is \(R_t = P_t/P_{t-1}-1\).

sp <- data.frame(Date = indices$Date, Price = indices$SP500)
sp <- sp[is.finite(sp$Price), ]
sp$R  <- simple_ret(sp$Price)        # simple net return
sp$r  <- log_ret(sp$Price)           # log return (next section)

kable(head(sp, 6), digits = 5,
      caption = "S&P 500: price, simple net return R_t, and log return r_t.")
S&P 500: price, simple net return R_t, and log return r_t.
Date Price R r
2000-01-03 1455.22 NA NA
2000-01-04 1399.42 -0.03834 -0.03910
2000-01-05 1402.11 0.00192 0.00192
2000-01-06 1403.45 0.00096 0.00096
2000-01-07 1441.47 0.02709 0.02673
2000-01-10 1457.60 0.01119 0.01113

Why we prefer returns to prices: returns are scale-free (a complete summary of the investment, independent of how many shares you hold) and far better behaved statistically. Compare the price level with the return series:

op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(3, 4, 2, 1))
plot(sp$Date, sp$Price, type = "l", col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "", ylab = "Price (adj.)", main = "S&P 500 price level (trending, non-stationary)")
plot(sp$Date, 100 * sp$R, type = "l", col = "firebrick",
     xlab = "", ylab = "Simple return (%)", main = "S&P 500 daily return (mean-reverting around 0)")
abline(h = 0, col = "grey50"); par(op)

3 Multi-period returns & compounding

Slides: “Asset Returns – Multi-period”. The \(k\)-period gross return is the product of one-period gross returns, \(1+R_t[k] = \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(1+R_{t-j})\).

Let us verify this identity on a concrete window — the 5 trading days after 2000-01-03 — by compounding daily returns and comparing to the direct price ratio \(P_t/P_{t-k}\).

w <- sp[2:6, ]                                  # five consecutive days
gross_prod <- prod(1 + w$R)                     # product of (1+R)
direct     <- sp$Price[6] / sp$Price[1]         # P_t / P_{t-k}
c(`compounded product` = gross_prod, `direct price ratio` = direct,
  difference = gross_prod - direct)
compounded product direct price ratio         difference 
      1.001635e+00       1.001635e+00      -2.220446e-16

They agree to numerical precision — compounding is the price ratio.

Annualisation. Holding for \(k\) years, the annualised return is the geometric mean \(\left[\prod (1+R)\right]^{1/k}-1\), well approximated by the arithmetic mean \(\frac1k\sum R\). On the full S&P sample (in trading days, ~252/yr):

R <- na.omit(sp$R)
geo  <- prod(1 + R)^(252 / length(R)) - 1       # exact geometric annualisation
arith <- mean(R) * 252                           # arithmetic approximation
log_based <- exp(mean(log(1 + R)) * 252) - 1     # via log returns
c(`geometric (exact)` = geo, `arithmetic approx` = arith,
  `log-mean based` = log_based)
geometric (exact) arithmetic approx    log-mean based 
       0.06463743        0.08127200        0.06463743

4 The compounding table and the number e

Slides: “Compounding Interest Rate” + “Continuous Compounding”. As the number of compounding periods \(m\to\infty\), \(\left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^{m}\to e^{r}\).

We reproduce the slide’s table from first principles (\(r = 10\%\), 1 year, $1):

m  <- c(Annual = 1, `Semi-annual` = 2, Quarterly = 4, Monthly = 12,
        Weekly = 52, Daily = 365)
r  <- 0.10
tab <- data.frame(
  Type            = names(m),
  `Payments`      = as.integer(m),
  `Rate/period`   = r / m,
  `Net value`     = (1 + r / m)^m,
  check.names = FALSE
)
tab <- rbind(tab, data.frame(Type = "Continuous", Payments = Inf,
             `Rate/period` = NA, `Net value` = exp(r), check.names = FALSE))
kable(tab, digits = 5, caption = "Effect of compounding frequency (r = 10% p.a.).")
Effect of compounding frequency (r = 10% p.a.).
Type Payments Rate/period Net value
Annual Annual 1 0.10000 1.10000
Semi-annual Semi-annual 2 0.05000 1.10250
Quarterly Quarterly 4 0.02500 1.10381
Monthly Monthly 12 0.00833 1.10471
Weekly Weekly 52 0.00192 1.10506
Daily Daily 365 0.00027 1.10516
1 Continuous Inf NA 1.10517

And the convergence to \(e^{0.1}=1.10517\) is visible when we let the frequency grow:

ms <- 2^(0:16)
plot(ms, (1 + r / ms)^ms, log = "x", type = "b", pch = 19, col = "darkgreen",
     xlab = "compounding periods per year (log scale)", ylab = "net value of $1",
     main = expression("Discrete compounding " %->% " continuous: (1 + r/m)^m -> e^r"))
abline(h = exp(r), lty = 2, col = "red")
text(2, exp(r), expression(e^{0.1}), pos = 1, col = "red")

5 Log returns and why we love them

Slides: “Continuously Compounding Return”. The log return \(r_t = \ln(1+R_t) = \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}\) is additive across time: \(r_t[k] = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j}\).

Additivity check — sum of daily log returns over the 5-day window equals the log of the price ratio:

c(`sum of daily log returns` = sum(w$r),
  `log of price ratio`       = log(direct),
  difference                 = sum(w$r) - log(direct))
sum of daily log returns       log of price ratio               difference 
            1.634159e-03             1.634159e-03            -3.610393e-16

Simple vs log. For small moves \(r_t \approx R_t\); they diverge only for large returns. The 45° line shows where they would be equal:

d <- na.omit(data.frame(R = sp$R, r = sp$r))
ggplot(d, aes(100 * R, 100 * r)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 0.6, colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Simple return R (%)", y = "Log return r (%)",
       title = "Daily S&P 500: log vs simple return",
       subtitle = "Near-identical for small moves; log < simple for big up-moves, log > simple for big down-moves")

The largest absolute gap occurs on the most extreme days:

d$gap <- 100 * (d$R - d$r)
kable(head(d[order(-abs(d$gap)), ], 5), digits = 3,
      caption = "Days where simple and log returns disagree most (gap in % pts).")
Days where simple and log returns disagree most (gap in % pts).
R r gap
5082 -0.120 -0.128 0.781
2208 0.116 0.110 0.623
2219 0.108 0.102 0.543
5080 -0.095 -0.100 0.483
2210 -0.090 -0.095 0.435

6 Portfolio returns

Slides: “Portfolio Return”. Simple returns aggregate exactly across assets, \(R_{p,t}=\sum_i w_i R_{i,t}\); log returns aggregate only approximately, \(r_{p,t}\approx\sum_i w_i r_{i,t}\).

Build an equal-weight portfolio of the five US stocks and compare the exact simple-return aggregation with the log approximation.

px  <- stocks[, c("AAPL","MSFT","AXP","CAT","SBUX")]
Rs  <- as.data.frame(lapply(px, function(p) simple_ret(p)))   # simple returns
rs  <- as.data.frame(lapply(px, function(p) log_ret(p)))       # log returns
wts <- rep(1/5, 5)                                  # equal weights

Rp        <- as.numeric(as.matrix(Rs) %*% wts)      # exact portfolio simple return
rp_exact  <- log(1 + Rp)                            # its true log return
rp_approx <- as.numeric(as.matrix(rs) %*% wts)      # weighted-average log return

cmp <- na.omit(data.frame(rp_exact = 100*rp_exact, rp_approx = 100*rp_approx))
cat(sprintf("Correlation(exact, approx) = %.6f;  max abs error = %.4f %%-pts\n",
            cor(cmp$rp_exact, cmp$rp_approx), max(abs(cmp$rp_exact - cmp$rp_approx))))
Correlation(exact, approx) = 0.999563;  max abs error = 3.4145 %-pts
ggplot(cmp, aes(rp_approx, rp_exact)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.6, colour = "darkorange") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "black") +
  labs(x = "Weighted-average log return  Σ wᵢ rᵢ (%)",
       y = "True portfolio log return  ln(1+Σ wᵢ Rᵢ) (%)",
       title = "Log-return aggregation is an excellent approximation at daily frequency")

7 Distributional properties of returns

Slides: “Variance, Skewness, Kurtosis” → “Moments of a Random Sample”. Mean, sd, skewness, and excess kurtosis (\(\mathcal K - 3\)).

We compute the four sample moments (in % per day) for every series, using the exact slide formulas. Note the near-universal positive excess kurtosis (fat tails) and negative skewness of equity returns.

all_px <- cbind(indices[ , -1], stocks[match(indices$Date, stocks$Date), -1])
ret_pct <- lapply(all_px, function(p) 100 * log_ret(p))   # % log returns

tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(ret_pct), function(nm) {
  s <- samp_stats(ret_pct[[nm]]); cbind(Series = nm, s)
}))
rownames(tab) <- NULL
kable(tab, digits = 3,
      caption = "Sample moments of daily % log returns (mean, sd, skew, excess kurtosis, min, max).")
Sample moments of daily % log returns (mean, sd, skew, excess kurtosis, min, max).
Series n mean sd skew exkurt min max
SP500 6388 0.028 1.217 -0.343 10.895 -12.765 10.957
NASDAQ 6388 0.032 1.557 -0.097 6.524 -13.149 13.255
DowJones 6388 0.025 1.154 -0.321 13.157 -13.842 10.764
FTSE100 6498 0.004 1.130 -0.371 8.540 -11.512 9.384
DAX 6580 0.015 1.410 -0.172 6.124 -13.055 10.797
CAC40 6645 0.002 1.370 -0.212 6.721 -13.098 10.595
Nikkei225 6138 0.006 1.439 -0.574 6.818 -13.234 9.737
HangSeng 6209 0.000 1.450 -0.051 7.147 -13.582 13.407
SSEComposite 6221 0.012 1.425 -0.339 5.679 -9.256 9.401
KOSPI 6209 0.032 1.463 -0.594 8.098 -12.849 11.284
Sensex 6143 0.033 1.352 -0.461 10.583 -14.102 15.990
AAPL 6388 0.088 2.505 -4.034 117.736 -73.125 14.262
MSFT 6388 0.045 1.889 -0.045 8.687 -15.945 17.869
AXP 6388 0.038 2.217 0.187 11.629 -19.352 19.789
CAT 6388 0.069 2.027 -0.093 4.587 -15.686 13.735
SBUX 6388 0.057 2.109 0.163 9.905 -20.363 21.911

8 The stylised facts (what the slides assert, made visible)

Slide: “Why i.i.d. normal is unrealistic” lists volatility clustering, fat tails, skewness, and serial dependence — here they are, on the S&P 500.

r <- 100 * na.omit(sp$r)
dts <- sp$Date[is.finite(sp$r)]

op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))

## (1) Volatility clustering: big moves cluster in time
plot(dts, r, type = "l", col = "grey30", xlab = "", ylab = "log return (%)",
     main = "(1) Volatility clustering")

## (2) Fat tails / skew: histogram with fitted Normal overlay
hist(r, breaks = 120, freq = FALSE, col = "grey85", border = "grey60",
     xlab = "log return (%)", main = "(2) Fat tails vs Normal")
curve(dnorm(x, mean(r), sd(r)), add = TRUE, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2)

## (3) Normal QQ-plot: departures in the tails
qqnorm(r, pch = 16, cex = 0.4, col = "steelblue",
       main = "(3) Normal Q-Q (tails bend off the line)")
qqline(r, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2)

## (4) Serial dependence: ACF of returns vs squared returns
a1 <- acf(r,   lag.max = 30, plot = FALSE)
a2 <- acf(r^2, lag.max = 30, plot = FALSE)
plot(a1$lag, a1$acf, type = "h", lwd = 2, col = "grey50", ylim = c(-0.05, 0.3),
     xlab = "lag", ylab = "ACF", main = "(4) ACF: returns (grey) vs squared (red)")
lines(a2$lag + 0.3, a2$acf, type = "h", lwd = 2, col = "firebrick")
abline(h = c(-1.96, 1.96)/sqrt(length(r)), lty = 2, col = "blue")
par(op)

Read-off: returns themselves are almost serially uncorrelated (grey, panel 4), but squared returns are strongly autocorrelated (red) — volatility is predictable even when the direction is not. This is the empirical seed of the ARCH/GARCH models in later chapters.

9 Testing normality

Slides: “Testing Skewness and Kurtosis” + “Jarque–Bera Test”. Under normality \(\hat{\mathcal S}\sim N(0,6/T)\), \(\hat{\mathcal K}-3\sim N(0,24/T)\), and \(\mathrm{JB}=\frac{\hat{\mathcal S}^2}{6/T}+\frac{(\hat{\mathcal K}-3)^2}{24/T}\to\chi^2_2\).

jb <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(ret_pct), function(nm)
  cbind(Series = nm, norm_tests(ret_pct[[nm]]))))
rownames(jb) <- NULL
kable(jb, digits = c(0,3,2,4,3,2,4,1,4),
      caption = "Skewness, excess-kurtosis and Jarque–Bera tests (p ≈ 0 ⇒ reject normality).")
Skewness, excess-kurtosis and Jarque–Bera tests (p ≈ 0 ⇒ reject normality).
Series skew t_skew p_skew exkurt t_kurt p_kurt JB p_JB
SP500 -0.343 -11.18 0.0000 10.895 177.75 0 31719.4 0
NASDAQ -0.097 -3.18 0.0015 6.524 106.43 0 11337.3 0
DowJones -0.321 -10.46 0.0000 13.157 214.65 0 46182.4 0
FTSE100 -0.371 -12.22 0.0000 8.540 140.52 0 19894.2 0
DAX -0.172 -5.68 0.0000 6.124 101.39 0 10313.1 0
CAC40 -0.212 -7.07 0.0000 6.721 111.83 0 12555.5 0
Nikkei225 -0.574 -18.37 0.0000 6.818 109.03 0 12225.9 0
HangSeng -0.051 -1.63 0.1030 7.147 114.96 0 13217.5 0
SSEComposite -0.339 -10.92 0.0000 5.679 91.44 0 8480.1 0
KOSPI -0.594 -19.09 0.0000 8.098 130.26 0 17332.1 0
Sensex -0.461 -14.74 0.0000 10.583 169.31 0 28882.6 0
AAPL -4.034 -131.63 0.0000 117.736 1920.82 0 3706866.4 0
MSFT -0.045 -1.46 0.1440 8.687 141.72 0 20086.4 0
AXP 0.187 6.09 0.0000 11.629 189.72 0 36029.1 0
CAT -0.093 -3.05 0.0023 4.587 74.84 0 5610.4 0
SBUX 0.163 5.31 0.0000 9.905 161.59 0 26139.0 0

Every single series rejects normality at any conventional level — the JB \(p\)-values are effectively zero, driven overwhelmingly by excess kurtosis. Cross-check our hand-coded JB against tseries::jarque.bera.test for the S&P:

jarque.bera.test(na.omit(sp$r))

    Jarque Bera Test

data:  na.omit(sp$r)
X-squared = 31490, df = 2, p-value < 2.2e-16
norm_tests(100 * sp$r)[, c("JB","p_JB")]

(The two JB values differ slightly because tseries uses the \(1/T\) moment scaling while the slides/Tsay use \(1/(T-1)\) — the conclusion is identical.)

10 Worked solution — Exercise 1

Worksheet Exercise 1: daily returns of AXP, CAT, SBUX over the last 5 years — summary statistics of simple % and log % returns, and a test that the mean log return is zero.

cutoff <- max(stocks$Date) - 365 * 5            # "last 5 years"
ex1 <- stocks[stocks$Date >= cutoff, c("Date","AXP","CAT","SBUX")]
cat("Window:", format(min(ex1$Date)), "to", format(max(ex1$Date)),
    "(", nrow(ex1), "trading days )\n")
Window: 2021-06-01 to 2026-05-29 ( 1255 trading days )

(1) & (3) Summary statistics of simple % and log % returns:

summ <- function(col, type = c("simple","log")) {
  p <- ex1[[col]]
  x <- if (match.arg(type) == "simple") 100 * simple_ret(p) else 100 * log_ret(p)
  cbind(Stock = col, Type = match.arg(type), samp_stats(x)[, c("mean","sd","skew","exkurt","min","max")])
}
res <- do.call(rbind, c(
  lapply(c("AXP","CAT","SBUX"), summ, type = "simple"),
  lapply(c("AXP","CAT","SBUX"), summ, type = "log")))
rownames(res) <- NULL
kable(res, digits = 3, caption = "Exercise 1: simple vs log % return statistics.")
Exercise 1: simple vs log % return statistics.
Stock Type mean sd skew exkurt min max
AXP simple 0.075 1.852 0.123 5.500 -9.965 13.384
CAT simple 0.128 1.923 0.465 3.806 -8.636 11.635
SBUX simple 0.018 1.991 1.337 24.444 -15.878 24.497
AXP log 0.057 1.851 -0.080 5.223 -10.498 12.561
CAT log 0.109 1.914 0.308 3.470 -9.031 11.006
SBUX log -0.001 1.973 0.668 19.914 -17.290 21.911

(4) Test \(H_0:\ \mathbb E[r]=0\) for each stock (one-sample \(t\)-test on log returns):

ttab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("AXP","CAT","SBUX"), function(col) {
  r <- na.omit(100 * log_ret(ex1[[col]]))
  tt <- t.test(r, mu = 0)
  data.frame(Stock = col, mean_pct = mean(r), t_stat = unname(tt$statistic),
             p_value = tt$p.value, reject_5pct = tt$p.value < 0.05)
}))
kable(ttab, digits = 4, caption = "H0: mean log return = 0 (5% level).")
H0: mean log return = 0 (5% level).
Stock mean_pct t_stat p_value reject_5pct
AXP 0.0574 1.0976 0.2726 FALSE
CAT 0.1095 2.0247 0.0431 TRUE
SBUX -0.0014 -0.0243 0.9806 FALSE

11 Worked solution — Exercise 2

Worksheet Exercise 2: monthly S&P 500, Jan 2000 – Dec 2025. (a) average annual log return; (b) value at end of 2025 of $1 invested at the start of 2010.

m <- sp_month[sp_month$Date >= as.Date("2000-01-01") &
              sp_month$Date <= as.Date("2025-12-31"), ]
m$r <- log_ret(m$SP500)                          # monthly log returns
mean_monthly <- mean(m$r, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_annual   <- 12 * mean_monthly                # log returns add => ×12

# (b) buy at start of 2010 (first month-end of 2010), sell at end of 2025
p0 <- m$SP500[format(m$Date, "%Y") == "2010"][1]
p1 <- tail(m$SP500[format(m$Date, "%Y") == "2025"], 1)
value_2025 <- 1 * p1 / p0

cat(sprintf("(a) Average annual log return, 2000-2025:  %.2f%%\n", 100 * avg_annual))
(a) Average annual log return, 2000-2025:  6.14%
cat(sprintf("(b) $1 invested start-2010 is worth        $%.2f at end-2025\n", value_2025))
(b) $1 invested start-2010 is worth        $6.37 at end-2025
cat(sprintf("    (that is a %.1f%% total / %.2f%% annualised simple return)\n",
            100 * (value_2025 - 1),
            100 * ((value_2025)^(1 / (2025 - 2010 + 1)) - 1)))
    (that is a 537.5% total / 12.27% annualised simple return)
plot(m$Date, m$SP500, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 1.5,
     xlab = "", ylab = "S&P 500 (month-end, adj.)",
     main = "S&P 500 monthly, 2000–2025")
abline(v = c(as.Date("2010-01-01"), as.Date("2025-12-31")), lty = 2, col = "firebrick")

12 Key takeaways

  1. Returns, not prices. Returns are scale-free and (roughly) stationary; prices trend and are not.
  2. Two definitions, one idea. Simple returns multiply across time and add across assets; log returns add across time and approximately add across assets. For daily data the two are almost interchangeable.
  3. Compounding → \(e\). Letting compounding frequency \(\to\infty\) gives continuous compounding and the log return.
  4. Returns are not Normal. Real equity returns show fat tails, mild negative skew, and volatility clustering; Jarque–Bera rejects normality for every series in our global sample. This motivates everything that follows in the course (GARCH, EVT, VaR).

Reproduce everything here with quarto render Day01_Supplement.qmd. Swap the running example by changing the column name ("SP500", "AAPL", …) — the code is column-agnostic.

Source Code
---
title: "FI 362 — Day 1 Supplement"
subtitle: "Asset Returns & Distributional Properties, Step by Step with Real Data"
author: "Mehman Ismayilli"
date: last-modified
date-format: long
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-width: 7
    fig-height: 4
    fig-align: center
    embed-resources: true
    df-print: paged
engine: knitr
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

> **How to use this notebook.** It mirrors the Day 1 slides
> (`FI362_Day01_FE`) section by section, but *computes* every concept on real
> market data instead of stating it abstractly. Read the slide, then run the
> matching block here. All data lives in the sibling `data/` folder and was
> built by `data/download_data.R` (daily Yahoo Finance prices, Jan 2000 –
> May 2026, adjusted for splits & dividends).

# Setup & data

```{r setup}
library(ggplot2)
library(moments)   # skewness(), kurtosis() -- we also code the Tsay versions by hand
library(tseries)   # jarque.bera.test()
library(zoo)
library(knitr)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

# Locate the data folder robustly (works whether rendered from this folder,
# the project root, or interactively).
data_dir <- local({
  cands <- c("../data", "data", file.path(getwd(), "data"))
  hit <- cands[file.exists(file.path(cands, "indices_adjclose.csv"))]
  if (!length(hit)) stop("Could not find the data/ folder. Run data/download_data.R first.")
  hit[1]
})

read_prices <- function(file) {
  df <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, file), check.names = FALSE)
  df$Date <- as.Date(df$Date)
  df
}

indices   <- read_prices("indices_adjclose.csv")   # daily, wide
stocks    <- read_prices("stocks_adjclose.csv")     # daily, wide
idx_month <- read_prices("indices_monthly.csv")     # month-end, wide
sp_month  <- read_prices("sp500_monthly.csv")       # month-end S&P 500
meta      <- read.csv(file.path(data_dir, "metadata.csv"))

kable(meta[, c("symbol","name","region","asset_class","rows","start","end")],
      caption = "The downloaded universe of global indices and stocks.")
```

We will use the **S&P 500** as our running example index and **AAPL** as our
running example stock, but every computation generalises to any column.

```{r helpers}
# --- log / simple return helpers -------------------------------------------
lag1       <- function(x) c(NA, head(x, -1))       # tiny one-step lag, no deps
simple_ret <- function(p) p / lag1(p) - 1          # R_t = P_t/P_{t-1} - 1
log_ret    <- function(p) c(NA, diff(log(p)))      # r_t = ln P_t - ln P_{t-1}

# --- sample moments EXACTLY as defined on the slides (Tsay, T-1 scaling) ----
samp_stats <- function(x) {
  x  <- x[is.finite(x)]
  T  <- length(x)
  m  <- mean(x)
  s  <- sqrt(sum((x - m)^2) / (T - 1))             # sample sd
  sk <- sum((x - m)^3) / ((T - 1) * s^3)           # sample skewness
  ku <- sum((x - m)^4) / ((T - 1) * s^4)           # sample kurtosis
  data.frame(n = T, mean = m, sd = s, skew = sk,
             exkurt = ku - 3, min = min(x), max = max(x))
}

# --- normality test statistics (slides: skew, kurtosis, Jarque-Bera) --------
norm_tests <- function(x) {
  x  <- x[is.finite(x)]; T <- length(x)
  st <- samp_stats(x)
  t_sk <- st$skew   / sqrt(6  / T)                 # H0: S = 0
  t_ku <- st$exkurt / sqrt(24 / T)                 # H0: K - 3 = 0
  JB   <- t_sk^2 + t_ku^2                           # = S^2/(6/T) + (K-3)^2/(24/T)
  data.frame(skew = st$skew, t_skew = t_sk, p_skew = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(t_sk))),
             exkurt = st$exkurt, t_kurt = t_ku, p_kurt = 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(t_ku))),
             JB = JB, p_JB = 1 - pchisq(JB, df = 2))
}
```

# From prices to returns {#sec-returns}

> *Slides: "Asset Returns – One period".* A one-period **simple gross return**
> is $1+R_t = P_t/P_{t-1}$; the **simple net return** is $R_t = P_t/P_{t-1}-1$.

```{r simple-returns}
sp <- data.frame(Date = indices$Date, Price = indices$SP500)
sp <- sp[is.finite(sp$Price), ]
sp$R  <- simple_ret(sp$Price)        # simple net return
sp$r  <- log_ret(sp$Price)           # log return (next section)

kable(head(sp, 6), digits = 5,
      caption = "S&P 500: price, simple net return R_t, and log return r_t.")
```

Why we prefer returns to prices: returns are **scale-free** (a complete summary
of the investment, independent of how many shares you hold) and far better
behaved statistically. Compare the price *level* with the return *series*:

```{r price-vs-return, fig.height=5}
op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 1), mar = c(3, 4, 2, 1))
plot(sp$Date, sp$Price, type = "l", col = "steelblue",
     xlab = "", ylab = "Price (adj.)", main = "S&P 500 price level (trending, non-stationary)")
plot(sp$Date, 100 * sp$R, type = "l", col = "firebrick",
     xlab = "", ylab = "Simple return (%)", main = "S&P 500 daily return (mean-reverting around 0)")
abline(h = 0, col = "grey50"); par(op)
```

# Multi-period returns & compounding {#sec-multiperiod}

> *Slides: "Asset Returns – Multi-period".* The $k$-period gross return is the
> **product** of one-period gross returns,
> $1+R_t[k] = \prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(1+R_{t-j})$.

Let us verify this identity on a concrete window — the 5 trading days after
2000-01-03 — by compounding daily returns and comparing to the direct
price ratio $P_t/P_{t-k}$.

```{r compound-check}
w <- sp[2:6, ]                                  # five consecutive days
gross_prod <- prod(1 + w$R)                     # product of (1+R)
direct     <- sp$Price[6] / sp$Price[1]         # P_t / P_{t-k}
c(`compounded product` = gross_prod, `direct price ratio` = direct,
  difference = gross_prod - direct)
```

They agree to numerical precision — compounding *is* the price ratio.

**Annualisation.** Holding for $k$ years, the annualised return is the geometric
mean $\left[\prod (1+R)\right]^{1/k}-1$, well approximated by the arithmetic mean
$\frac1k\sum R$. On the full S&P sample (in trading days, ~252/yr):

```{r annualise}
R <- na.omit(sp$R)
geo  <- prod(1 + R)^(252 / length(R)) - 1       # exact geometric annualisation
arith <- mean(R) * 252                           # arithmetic approximation
log_based <- exp(mean(log(1 + R)) * 252) - 1     # via log returns
c(`geometric (exact)` = geo, `arithmetic approx` = arith,
  `log-mean based` = log_based)
```

# The compounding table and the number *e* {#sec-continuous}

> *Slides: "Compounding Interest Rate" + "Continuous Compounding".* As the number
> of compounding periods $m\to\infty$, $\left(1+\frac{r}{m}\right)^{m}\to e^{r}$.

We reproduce the slide's table from first principles ($r = 10\%$, 1 year, \$1):

```{r compounding-table}
m  <- c(Annual = 1, `Semi-annual` = 2, Quarterly = 4, Monthly = 12,
        Weekly = 52, Daily = 365)
r  <- 0.10
tab <- data.frame(
  Type            = names(m),
  `Payments`      = as.integer(m),
  `Rate/period`   = r / m,
  `Net value`     = (1 + r / m)^m,
  check.names = FALSE
)
tab <- rbind(tab, data.frame(Type = "Continuous", Payments = Inf,
             `Rate/period` = NA, `Net value` = exp(r), check.names = FALSE))
kable(tab, digits = 5, caption = "Effect of compounding frequency (r = 10% p.a.).")
```

And the convergence to $e^{0.1}=`r round(exp(0.1),5)`$ is visible when we let the
frequency grow:

```{r e-limit}
ms <- 2^(0:16)
plot(ms, (1 + r / ms)^ms, log = "x", type = "b", pch = 19, col = "darkgreen",
     xlab = "compounding periods per year (log scale)", ylab = "net value of $1",
     main = expression("Discrete compounding " %->% " continuous: (1 + r/m)^m -> e^r"))
abline(h = exp(r), lty = 2, col = "red")
text(2, exp(r), expression(e^{0.1}), pos = 1, col = "red")
```

# Log returns and why we love them {#sec-logret}

> *Slides: "Continuously Compounding Return".* The **log return**
> $r_t = \ln(1+R_t) = \ln P_t - \ln P_{t-1}$ is **additive across time**:
> $r_t[k] = \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} r_{t-j}$.

**Additivity check** — sum of daily log returns over the 5-day window equals the
log of the price ratio:

```{r logret-additive}
c(`sum of daily log returns` = sum(w$r),
  `log of price ratio`       = log(direct),
  difference                 = sum(w$r) - log(direct))
```

**Simple vs log.** For small moves $r_t \approx R_t$; they diverge only for large
returns. The 45° line shows where they would be equal:

```{r simple-vs-log}
d <- na.omit(data.frame(R = sp$R, r = sp$r))
ggplot(d, aes(100 * R, 100 * r)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25, size = 0.6, colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Simple return R (%)", y = "Log return r (%)",
       title = "Daily S&P 500: log vs simple return",
       subtitle = "Near-identical for small moves; log < simple for big up-moves, log > simple for big down-moves")
```

The largest absolute gap occurs on the most extreme days:

```{r gap}
d$gap <- 100 * (d$R - d$r)
kable(head(d[order(-abs(d$gap)), ], 5), digits = 3,
      caption = "Days where simple and log returns disagree most (gap in % pts).")
```

# Portfolio returns {#sec-portfolio}

> *Slides: "Portfolio Return".* Simple returns aggregate **exactly** across
> assets, $R_{p,t}=\sum_i w_i R_{i,t}$; log returns aggregate only
> **approximately**, $r_{p,t}\approx\sum_i w_i r_{i,t}$.

Build an equal-weight portfolio of the five US stocks and compare the *exact*
simple-return aggregation with the *log* approximation.

```{r portfolio}
px  <- stocks[, c("AAPL","MSFT","AXP","CAT","SBUX")]
Rs  <- as.data.frame(lapply(px, function(p) simple_ret(p)))   # simple returns
rs  <- as.data.frame(lapply(px, function(p) log_ret(p)))       # log returns
wts <- rep(1/5, 5)                                  # equal weights

Rp        <- as.numeric(as.matrix(Rs) %*% wts)      # exact portfolio simple return
rp_exact  <- log(1 + Rp)                            # its true log return
rp_approx <- as.numeric(as.matrix(rs) %*% wts)      # weighted-average log return

cmp <- na.omit(data.frame(rp_exact = 100*rp_exact, rp_approx = 100*rp_approx))
cat(sprintf("Correlation(exact, approx) = %.6f;  max abs error = %.4f %%-pts\n",
            cor(cmp$rp_exact, cmp$rp_approx), max(abs(cmp$rp_exact - cmp$rp_approx))))

ggplot(cmp, aes(rp_approx, rp_exact)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.6, colour = "darkorange") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "black") +
  labs(x = "Weighted-average log return  Σ wᵢ rᵢ (%)",
       y = "True portfolio log return  ln(1+Σ wᵢ Rᵢ) (%)",
       title = "Log-return aggregation is an excellent approximation at daily frequency")
```

# Distributional properties of returns {#sec-moments}

> *Slides: "Variance, Skewness, Kurtosis" → "Moments of a Random Sample".*
> Mean, sd, skewness, and **excess** kurtosis ($\mathcal K - 3$).

We compute the four sample moments (in % per day) for every series, using the
exact slide formulas. Note the **near-universal positive excess kurtosis**
(fat tails) and **negative skewness** of equity returns.

```{r moments-table}
all_px <- cbind(indices[ , -1], stocks[match(indices$Date, stocks$Date), -1])
ret_pct <- lapply(all_px, function(p) 100 * log_ret(p))   # % log returns

tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(ret_pct), function(nm) {
  s <- samp_stats(ret_pct[[nm]]); cbind(Series = nm, s)
}))
rownames(tab) <- NULL
kable(tab, digits = 3,
      caption = "Sample moments of daily % log returns (mean, sd, skew, excess kurtosis, min, max).")
```

# The stylised facts (what the slides assert, made visible) {#sec-stylised}

> *Slide: "Why i.i.d. normal is unrealistic"* lists volatility clustering, fat
> tails, skewness, and serial dependence — here they are, on the S&P 500.

```{r stylised, fig.height=6}
r <- 100 * na.omit(sp$r)
dts <- sp$Date[is.finite(sp$r)]

op <- par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 2.5, 1))

## (1) Volatility clustering: big moves cluster in time
plot(dts, r, type = "l", col = "grey30", xlab = "", ylab = "log return (%)",
     main = "(1) Volatility clustering")

## (2) Fat tails / skew: histogram with fitted Normal overlay
hist(r, breaks = 120, freq = FALSE, col = "grey85", border = "grey60",
     xlab = "log return (%)", main = "(2) Fat tails vs Normal")
curve(dnorm(x, mean(r), sd(r)), add = TRUE, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2)

## (3) Normal QQ-plot: departures in the tails
qqnorm(r, pch = 16, cex = 0.4, col = "steelblue",
       main = "(3) Normal Q-Q (tails bend off the line)")
qqline(r, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2)

## (4) Serial dependence: ACF of returns vs squared returns
a1 <- acf(r,   lag.max = 30, plot = FALSE)
a2 <- acf(r^2, lag.max = 30, plot = FALSE)
plot(a1$lag, a1$acf, type = "h", lwd = 2, col = "grey50", ylim = c(-0.05, 0.3),
     xlab = "lag", ylab = "ACF", main = "(4) ACF: returns (grey) vs squared (red)")
lines(a2$lag + 0.3, a2$acf, type = "h", lwd = 2, col = "firebrick")
abline(h = c(-1.96, 1.96)/sqrt(length(r)), lty = 2, col = "blue")
par(op)
```

Read-off: returns themselves are almost serially uncorrelated (grey, panel 4),
but **squared** returns are strongly autocorrelated (red) — volatility is
predictable even when the direction is not. This is the empirical seed of the
ARCH/GARCH models in later chapters.

# Testing normality {#sec-tests}

> *Slides: "Testing Skewness and Kurtosis" + "Jarque–Bera Test".* Under
> normality $\hat{\mathcal S}\sim N(0,6/T)$, $\hat{\mathcal K}-3\sim N(0,24/T)$,
> and $\mathrm{JB}=\frac{\hat{\mathcal S}^2}{6/T}+\frac{(\hat{\mathcal K}-3)^2}{24/T}\to\chi^2_2$.

```{r jb-table}
jb <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(ret_pct), function(nm)
  cbind(Series = nm, norm_tests(ret_pct[[nm]]))))
rownames(jb) <- NULL
kable(jb, digits = c(0,3,2,4,3,2,4,1,4),
      caption = "Skewness, excess-kurtosis and Jarque–Bera tests (p ≈ 0 ⇒ reject normality).")
```

Every single series rejects normality at any conventional level — the JB
$p$-values are effectively zero, driven overwhelmingly by **excess kurtosis**.
Cross-check our hand-coded JB against `tseries::jarque.bera.test` for the S&P:

```{r jb-crosscheck}
jarque.bera.test(na.omit(sp$r))
norm_tests(100 * sp$r)[, c("JB","p_JB")]
```

(The two JB values differ slightly because `tseries` uses the $1/T$ moment
scaling while the slides/Tsay use $1/(T-1)$ — the conclusion is identical.)

# Worked solution — Exercise 1 {#sec-ex1}

> *Worksheet Exercise 1:* daily returns of **AXP, CAT, SBUX** over the last 5
> years — summary statistics of simple % and log % returns, and a test that the
> mean log return is zero.

```{r ex1-data}
cutoff <- max(stocks$Date) - 365 * 5            # "last 5 years"
ex1 <- stocks[stocks$Date >= cutoff, c("Date","AXP","CAT","SBUX")]
cat("Window:", format(min(ex1$Date)), "to", format(max(ex1$Date)),
    "(", nrow(ex1), "trading days )\n")
```

**(1) & (3) Summary statistics** of simple % and log % returns:

```{r ex1-stats}
summ <- function(col, type = c("simple","log")) {
  p <- ex1[[col]]
  x <- if (match.arg(type) == "simple") 100 * simple_ret(p) else 100 * log_ret(p)
  cbind(Stock = col, Type = match.arg(type), samp_stats(x)[, c("mean","sd","skew","exkurt","min","max")])
}
res <- do.call(rbind, c(
  lapply(c("AXP","CAT","SBUX"), summ, type = "simple"),
  lapply(c("AXP","CAT","SBUX"), summ, type = "log")))
rownames(res) <- NULL
kable(res, digits = 3, caption = "Exercise 1: simple vs log % return statistics.")
```

**(4) Test $H_0:\ \mathbb E[r]=0$** for each stock (one-sample $t$-test on log
returns):

```{r ex1-ttest}
ttab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("AXP","CAT","SBUX"), function(col) {
  r <- na.omit(100 * log_ret(ex1[[col]]))
  tt <- t.test(r, mu = 0)
  data.frame(Stock = col, mean_pct = mean(r), t_stat = unname(tt$statistic),
             p_value = tt$p.value, reject_5pct = tt$p.value < 0.05)
}))
kable(ttab, digits = 4, caption = "H0: mean log return = 0 (5% level).")
```

# Worked solution — Exercise 2 {#sec-ex2}

> *Worksheet Exercise 2:* monthly **S&P 500**, Jan 2000 – Dec 2025.
> (a) average annual log return; (b) value at end of 2025 of \$1 invested at the
> start of 2010.

```{r ex2}
m <- sp_month[sp_month$Date >= as.Date("2000-01-01") &
              sp_month$Date <= as.Date("2025-12-31"), ]
m$r <- log_ret(m$SP500)                          # monthly log returns
mean_monthly <- mean(m$r, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_annual   <- 12 * mean_monthly                # log returns add => ×12

# (b) buy at start of 2010 (first month-end of 2010), sell at end of 2025
p0 <- m$SP500[format(m$Date, "%Y") == "2010"][1]
p1 <- tail(m$SP500[format(m$Date, "%Y") == "2025"], 1)
value_2025 <- 1 * p1 / p0

cat(sprintf("(a) Average annual log return, 2000-2025:  %.2f%%\n", 100 * avg_annual))
cat(sprintf("(b) $1 invested start-2010 is worth        $%.2f at end-2025\n", value_2025))
cat(sprintf("    (that is a %.1f%% total / %.2f%% annualised simple return)\n",
            100 * (value_2025 - 1),
            100 * ((value_2025)^(1 / (2025 - 2010 + 1)) - 1)))
```

```{r ex2-plot}
plot(m$Date, m$SP500, type = "l", col = "steelblue", lwd = 1.5,
     xlab = "", ylab = "S&P 500 (month-end, adj.)",
     main = "S&P 500 monthly, 2000–2025")
abline(v = c(as.Date("2010-01-01"), as.Date("2025-12-31")), lty = 2, col = "firebrick")
```

# Key takeaways {#sec-takeaways}

1. **Returns, not prices.** Returns are scale-free and (roughly) stationary;
   prices trend and are not.
2. **Two definitions, one idea.** Simple returns *multiply* across time and *add*
   across assets; log returns *add* across time and *approximately* add across
   assets. For daily data the two are almost interchangeable.
3. **Compounding → $e$.** Letting compounding frequency $\to\infty$ gives
   continuous compounding and the log return.
4. **Returns are not Normal.** Real equity returns show fat tails, mild negative
   skew, and volatility clustering; Jarque–Bera rejects normality for *every*
   series in our global sample. This motivates everything that follows in the
   course (GARCH, EVT, VaR).

*Reproduce everything here with `quarto render Day01_Supplement.qmd`. Swap the
running example by changing the column name (`"SP500"`, `"AAPL"`, …) — the code
is column-agnostic.*
